metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `authorize-source` job's checkout step must use `fetch-depth: 0` (full history) so that `git merge-base --is-ancestor` can resolve true ancestry beyond a shallow graft boundary — as the codebase already does in `reconcile-bridge.yml` and `verify-identity.yaml`.
findings.finding: The `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` ancestry check will fail spuriously (exit 1 = not-ancestor) when `actions/checkout@v7` uses its default shallow clone (`fetch-depth: 1`), because git cannot resolve ancestry beyond the grafted boundary. The codebase itself documents this exact failure mode (reconcile-bridge.yml:83-88: 'A shallow graft defeats git's merge-base computation'; verify-identity.yaml also uses `fetch-depth: 0`). The plan does not specify that the `authorize-source` checkout must use `fetch-depth: 0` (or an equivalent `git fetch --unshallow`). On a shallow clone, a tag on a valid main-ancestor commit (e.g., 10 commits behind HEAD) would fail the gate — producing a false-negative that blocks a legitimate release — while a tag on main's exact tip would pass only by coincidence.
findings.location: What section — 'Resolve the release SHA explicitly and fetch the protected `main` ref. Prove the release SHA is reachable from reviewed `main` per policy (`git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the `authorize-source` job's `actions/checkout` step, add `fetch-depth: 0`. Then explicitly fetch `origin/main` with `git fetch origin main` to ensure the ref is present. Example:
```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@v7
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0
- name: Fetch origin/main for ancestry check
  run: git fetch origin main
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `fetch-depth: 0` (or an explicit full-history `git fetch --unshallow`) in the `authorize-source` job's checkout step, consistent with the project's existing `test_reconcile_workflow_no_shallow_rebase.py` invariant that shallow history defeats `git merge-base`.
findings.finding: The plan proposes `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` as the ancestry proof in `authorize-source`, but does not specify `fetch-depth: 0` for that job's checkout. The existing `release.yml` uses `actions/checkout@v7` with no `fetch-depth` override (GitHub Actions default is `fetch-depth: 1`, a shallow clone). The project's own prior art already codifies that a shallow checkout defeats `git merge-base` (bug f193, `test_reconcile_workflow_no_shallow_rebase.py`, and the `fetch-depth: 0` invariant enforced in `reconcile-bridge.yml` and `verify-identity.yaml`). Without `fetch-depth: 0`, the ancestry check will fail spuriously for any tag commit that is not the literal tip of the shallow fetch, silently producing false-negatives (the gate blocks legitimate releases) or, if `git fetch origin main` is used to supplement history, false-positives if that fetch is also shallow.
findings.location: What section — authorize-source job description; `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`
findings.suggested_fix: In the `authorize-source` job's `actions/checkout` step, add `with: fetch-depth: 0`. Alternatively, after a shallow checkout, run `git fetch --unshallow origin` before the `git merge-base` call. Add a contract test (analogous to `test_reconcile_workflow_no_shallow_rebase.py`) asserting that `release.yml`'s `authorize-source` job checkout uses `fetch-depth: 0`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `fetch-depth: 0` (full history) on the `authorize-source` job's `actions/checkout` step, AND add an explicit `git fetch origin main` after checkout so `origin/main` is populated — required for `git merge-base --is-ancestor` to work on any tagged commit that is not the current HEAD of main.
findings.finding: `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` requires full git history to work correctly, but the plan does not specify `fetch-depth: 0` (or an explicit `--unshallow`) for the `authorize-source` job's checkout. The default `actions/checkout@v7` shallow clone (depth=1) only fetches the tip commit; `merge-base` on a shallow graph will return exit-code 1 ("not an ancestor") for any tagged commit that is not the literal HEAD of `origin/main` at clone time, causing valid releases to fail the gate. The repo already has `test_reconcile_workflow_no_shallow_rebase.py` which asserts `fetch-depth: 0` for workflows using merge-base — but that test only covers the two reconcile workflows, not `release.yml`. The plan's 'What' section describes the step but is silent on this prerequisite.
findings.location: What section — `authorize-source` job description: 'Resolve the release SHA explicitly and fetch the protected `main` ref. Prove the release SHA is reachable from reviewed `main` per policy (`git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`…)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the `authorize-source` job's checkout step, add:
```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@v7
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0   # full history required for git merge-base
- name: Fetch protected main ref
  run: git fetch origin main
```
Then update (or extend) `test_reconcile_workflow_no_shallow_rebase.py` — or add a parallel test for `release.yml` — to assert that `authorize-source` also uses `fetch-depth: 0`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `authorize-source` behaves on `workflow_dispatch` runs: either (a) skip the tag-parsing portion of the lockstep check when `github.event_name != 'push'` (since publish jobs already gate on the push condition), or (b) require the dispatcher to supply the version as an input and validate against it, consistent with the stated uv/pydantic pattern.
findings.finding: The `authorize-source` job is not scoped to the `push` trigger only. When the workflow runs via `workflow_dispatch` (the existing dry-run path, `release.yml:21`), `GITHUB_SHA` points to the dispatched commit (typically the current main HEAD, which trivially passes ancestry), but there is no `refs/tags/v*` ref — so the version-lockstep step cannot parse `tag_without_v` from `github.ref_name`, causing it to either produce an empty string or fail with a confusing error before the version comparison. The plan says 'runs before any OIDC-enabled job' but does not specify how `authorize-source` behaves on `workflow_dispatch` runs.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a source-authorization job that runs before any OIDC-enabled job'; Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml — new authorize-source job`; release.yml:17-21 (on: push tags v* AND workflow_dispatch)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen ancestry policy explicitly in the plan (e.g., 'Use `git merge-base --is-ancestor` so that exact-tip tags pass and future hotfix tags from a branch cut off main also pass; exact-equality is not required') so the executor does not need to make a security-relevant design choice.
findings.finding: The ancestry policy is left as an open choice for the executor: the plan offers two semantically different options ('git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main' OR 'exact-equality to the main tip if the project prefers') without deciding between them. An executor implementing the gate must choose a policy that has real semantic consequences (ancestor allows pre-release branches; exact-equality forbids them), and the plan defers this choice with 'if the project prefers'.
findings.location: What section, bullet 2 — 'Prove the release SHA is reachable from reviewed main per policy (git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main, or exact-equality to the main tip if the project prefers).'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'or exact-equality to the main tip if the project prefers' with a firm decision: 'Use `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$RELEASE_SHA" origin/main` (ancestor check). Exact-equality is not required and would break legitimate hotfix releases.' If exact-equality is truly preferred, state that explicitly and add a note that hotfix branches are not supported.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the evidence artifact is produced — e.g., 'the authorize-source job uploads (or prints to the job summary) a record containing the approved SHA, resolved tag, all manifest versions, and artifact hashes, and the run is verifiable by inspecting that output'.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a deliverable — 'Record the approved SHA, tag, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence (e.g. a job summary / uploaded evidence file)' — that has no corresponding acceptance criterion. No AC verifies that this evidence artifact is produced or that it contains the required fields.
findings.location: What section, last bullet ('Record the approved SHA…'); Acceptance Criteria section (all AC items)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item such as: '[ ] The `authorize-source` job emits a job summary (or uploads an evidence file) that records the approved SHA, the tag, the pyproject.toml version, the server.json version, each packages[].version, and (after build) the artifact hashes — verified by inspecting the Actions run summary for a tagged commit that passes the gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to one ancestry policy (recommend is-ancestor, which matches AC1 and allows the 'Release X.Y.Z' commit to be tagged after subsequent commits land on main) and remove the 'or exact-equality' alternative from the What section to eliminate implementer ambiguity.
findings.finding: The ancestry-check policy is left ambiguous between two semantically distinct options. The What section says `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main`, **or** 'exact-equality to the `main` tip if the project prefers'. But AC1 says 'fails the run when the SHA is not reachable from `main`' — which is ancestry semantics only (exact-equality is a strict subset). An implementer reading the plan cannot tell which to implement: exact-equality blocks any tag that isn't the current HEAD of main (forcing a tag-then-immediately-push discipline); ancestry allows tags on any reviewed ancestor. These have materially different operational properties, and the plan needs to commit to one rather than presenting both as equivalent alternatives.
findings.location: What section — 'git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main, or exact-equality to the `main` tip if the project prefers'; AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in the `authorize-source` step or a comment in `release.yml`) that the set of checked version sources is exhaustive-as-of-now, and add a note in `docs/releasing.md` instructing authors to update the lockstep assertion whenever a new version-carrying manifest is introduced.
findings.finding: The version-lockstep assertion step hardcodes exactly four version sources (tag, pyproject.toml, server.json top-level, server.json.packages[*].version). If the project later adds a new manifest or version carrier (e.g., a Dockerfile label, a second server.json package, or a new registry manifest), the assertion step must be manually discovered and updated — there is no plan to make the set of checked sources self-describing or extensible.
findings.location: ## What — 'Parse the tag and every manifest and assert exact equality before build'; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC item 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment block in the lockstep step listing the checked sources and a one-line instruction: '# If you add a new manifest with a version field, extend this check.' Also add a sentence to docs/releasing.md: 'When adding a new release artifact that carries a version, update the lockstep assertion in authorize-source.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `github_repository_environment` (or `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy`) Terraform resource for the `pypi` environment with `reviewers` set, so the required-reviewer gate is IaC-managed, plan-visible, and drift-detectable — rather than operator-attested only.
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub Actions environment's required-reviewers setting is a load-bearing security control (described as 'independently-approved environment' and one of 'the load-bearing controls') but is not represented in any IaC — no `github_repository_environment` or equivalent Terraform resource exists. The plan specifies it only as an operator-attested manual step (AC line 6), making the control non-reproducible as code and invisible to `terraform plan`, so it can be silently removed out-of-band with no automated detection.
findings.location: AC line 6 ('operator-attested') and 'What' section ('independently-approved deployment environment (required reviewers on the `pypi` environment')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` a `github_repository_environment` resource (provider `integrations/github` supports this): set `environment = "pypi"`, `deployment_branch_policy { protected_branches = false; custom_branch_policies = true }` and a `reviewers { teams = [...] }` or `reviewers { users = [...] }` block. Then add a matching `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` to restrict to `v*` tags. This makes the control reproducible, plan-visible, and subject to the same Gerrit two-vote gate as all other IaC changes. If a `github_environment` resource risks importing a conflict with an existing manually-created environment, document the `terraform import` step in the runbook alongside the existing mirror-lock import note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the `mcp-publisher` download to a specific, immutable release version (not `releases/latest`) and verify the downloaded archive against a recorded SHA-256 digest before execution, since the binary receives a live `id-token: write` OIDC credential on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job (which holds `id-token: write`) fetches the `mcp-publisher` binary at runtime via `curl -L .../releases/latest/download/...` — a mutable floating reference with no integrity check. The plan adds `authorize-source` as a provenance gate and explicitly scopes `release.yml` as a deliverable, but does not specify that the binary download be pinned to a known version and verified against a recorded SHA-256. A substituted or compromised binary on this path can exfiltrate the OIDC token used to publish to the MCP Registry.
findings.location: .github/workflows/release.yml:129 (mcp_registry job, Install mcp-publisher step); plan 'What' section / 'Scope' section (release.yml is an explicit deliverable)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `Install mcp-publisher` step, replace `releases/latest/download` with `releases/download/vX.Y.Z` for a pinned version, and add a `sha256sum --check` step after extraction. Record the expected digest in the plan/workflow (e.g., a comment or a separate digest file). Update the pin when a new `mcp-publisher` version is needed, treating it like any other pinned dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff behavior for the `git fetch origin main` step in `authorize-source` (e.g., `--retry 3` via `git` or a step-level `retry` action) and document the 'transient fetch failure → re-run workflow' recovery path in `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The `authorize-source` job performs a `git fetch origin main` (external network I/O) with no specified retry or backoff. A transient GitHub git-protocol failure causes a false-negative gate failure, blocking a legitimate release and requiring a manual re-run with no guidance in the runbook.
findings.location: ## What — 'Resolve the release SHA explicitly and fetch the protected `main` ref.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `uses: nick-fields/retry@v3` wrapper (or `git fetch --retry`) around the fetch step with 3 attempts and 10-second backoff, and add a sentence to `docs/releasing.md`: 'If `authorize-source` fails with a network error on the git fetch, re-run the workflow — no tag change is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `docs/releasing.md` (a) what the approver must verify before approving (e.g., confirm the evidence summary names the expected tag/SHA/versions), (b) that the release-evidence artifact is REQUIRED (not advisory) so the approver has something to check, and (c) the recovery path if the approval window times out or is rejected (e.g., 'retag at the same SHA and re-run').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a required-reviewer approval step on the `pypi` environment (a user-facing interaction surface), but does not specify what information the approver is shown, what they are expected to verify before approving, or what the rejection/timeout recovery path looks like — leaving the 'independently-approved environment' potentially a rubber-stamp with no decision criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC item 6 (operator-attested); 'What' section — 'make publish depend on an independently-approved deployment environment'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, change 'e.g. a job summary / uploaded evidence file' to 'a mandatory uploaded evidence artifact (job summary + artifact) that the approver must reference before approving'. Add a one-paragraph approver checklist to the docs/releasing.md specification, and add an AC that the evidence artifact is present and linked in the environment approval UI.

findings.finding: The plan asserts the tag ruleset (`refs/tags/v*`) in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` can restrict tag creation/update/deletion to a 'controlled release identity', but the existing `main.tf` explicitly documents (lines 23-28) that the tag_lock resource was REMOVED because there was no viable bypass actor for tag writes — the only supported bypass type currently is `DeployKey` (the Gerrit replication key). The plan never identifies what `actor_type`/`actor_id` would form the bypass for a 'release identity' (human tagger or GitHub Actions), leaving an unverified capability assertion at the heart of AC #4.
findings.location: What section — 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`'; AC #4; `infra/terraform-github/main.tf:23-28`
findings.suggested_fix: Consult the GitHub rulesets API and the `integrations/github` provider changelog to confirm whether an `actor_type = "Integration"` (GitHub Actions itself, integration_id=2) or a personal-access-token bypass exists. If no suitable bypass actor is available, the tag-ruleset AC should be scoped to only `deletion` + `non_fast_forward` rules (preventing tag deletion/force-push) while leaving tag creation open to the existing model, and the plan text should be updated to reflect this reduced constraint.

findings.finding: The plan's AC4 requires adding a `github_repository_ruleset` for `refs/tags/v*` in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, but `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` explicitly asserts that this resource MUST NOT exist (`assert 'resource "github_repository_ruleset" "tag_lock"' not in tf`, `assert 'target      = "tag"' not in tf`, `assert 'include = ["refs/tags/**"]' not in tf`). Implementing the plan as written will break a passing test, causing the plan's own final AC (`make test` passes) to fail.
findings.location: AC4 / infra/terraform-github/main.tf / tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` to allow a `tag_lock` ruleset when the bypass actor is a release identity (the WS7 comment in main.tf explicitly anticipated this: "To re-lock tags later, re-add it plus a release-tag path"); document the override rationale in the test.'

findings.finding: AC4 requires adding a `github_repository_ruleset` with `target = "tag"` to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, but the existing contract test `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` (tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46) explicitly asserts that `'resource "github_repository_ruleset" "tag_lock"' not in tf` and `'target = "tag"' not in tf`. Implementing AC4 without updating or superseding this test will cause `make test` to fail, directly contradicting AC7 ('make test passes').
findings.location: AC4 (infra/terraform-github/main.tf), tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:39-46
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and AC4, add: 'Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` — replace the `assert "tag_lock" not in tf` negative assertion with a positive contract test that pins the new tag ruleset's rules and bypass_actors, preserving the spirit of the contract (lock shape is auditable) while allowing the new resource.'

findings.finding: The plan requires a Terraform ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` that restricts tag creation/update/deletion to a 'controlled release identity', but never declares what that identity is (a specific GitHub team, bot account, service account, or deploy key). An implementer left to choose may grant bypass to all repository admins or all org members, recreating the exact gap the plan aims to close.
findings.location: Scope section / `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` bullet; AC: 'defines a ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope/What section and AC-4 naming the exact principal: e.g., 'The ruleset bypass list contains exactly one entry: the `release-bot` GitHub App / the `release` team with no admin members / the Gerrit replication deploy key — no individual user accounts and no admin-role bypass.' Reference this principal in the Terraform AC so the diff is reviewable against the stated constraint.

findings.finding: The 'release identity' that will be granted tag-push rights in the Terraform ruleset is never defined. The plan says 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never names what that identity is (a machine account, a GitHub App, the Gerrit deploy key, a specific org role, or an admin team).
findings.location: Scope — `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`; Acceptance Criteria AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The release identity is the `gerrit-replication` deploy-key bot account (or the `release-bot` GitHub App — whichever is used for the Gerrit→GitHub mirror); the Terraform ruleset's `bypass_actors` block should reference that actor's node ID.' If the identity is TBD, pick a concrete default (e.g. a named admin team) and state it.

findings.finding: The "release identity" — the GitHub actor that the Terraform ruleset grants tag create/update/delete rights to — is referenced in Scope, What, and AC but is never defined. It is a load-bearing cross-component coupling: the Terraform `main.tf` ruleset must name a concrete principal (user login, team slug, or app), and the human/automation that pushes tags must be that same principal, and the `authorize-source` workflow job must be reachable only via that identity's tag push. Without specifying what the identity is, the Terraform change and the workflow gate may be implemented inconsistently.
findings.location: Scope — `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` bullet; What — 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity'; AC — 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Release identity' subsection under What (or a table in `docs/releasing.md`) specifying e.g. 'The release identity is the GitHub team `org/release-managers`; the Terraform `bypass_actors` block will reference this team slug; human releasers must be members of this team to push a `v*` tag.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies adding a Terraform ruleset over `refs/tags/v*` that restricts tag writes to 'the release identity', but never defines what that identity is. The existing `main.tf` bypass mechanism is a `DeployKey` with `bypass_mode = always` — that key is used for Gerrit replication, not for CI-triggered tag creation. GitHub Actions runners are not a DeployKey; they authenticate as the `GITHUB_TOKEN` installation. Without defining the bypass actor (e.g., a GitHub App, a specific service account, or an Actions environment token), the Terraform ruleset either locks out tag creation entirely or the implementation will have to guess the bypass identity, possibly defeating the security goal.
findings.location: What — fifth bullet; AC line 4 (`infra/terraform-github/main.tf` defines a ruleset over `refs/tags/v*`)

findings.finding: The 'release identity' referenced as the bypass actor for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` is never defined anywhere in the plan. The existing module only knows a single bypass actor type (DeployKey for Gerrit replication). The plan names no actor type, actor ID, or mechanism for what identity may create/update/delete `v*` tags — leaving the implementer without a specification for the core security primitive the AC depends on.
findings.location: What section ('Restrict refs/tags/v* creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in infra/terraform-github/main.tf') and AC4 ('restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Release identity' subsection under 'What' that names the actor: e.g., if a human maintainer will push tags, specify a named GitHub user bypass (`actor_type = "RepositoryRole"` or a specific user); if CI will push tags via the deploy key, confirm that the existing Gerrit deploy key is also the release key and document that; if the tag push stays manual with no additional Terraform enforcement (the gate being the workflow check instead), clarify that the Terraform ruleset is out of scope for this story.

findings.finding: The plan's AC4 and What section specify 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never define what GitHub entity constitutes the 'release identity' — no actor_type (DeployKey, App, OrganizationAdmin, RepositoryRole?), actor_id, or GitHub identity is named. The `bypass_actors` block of a `github_repository_ruleset` requires concrete values; without them, the Terraform resource cannot be implemented, and a wrong choice could lock out the tag-push path entirely (replicating the same failure mode the existing main_lock comments warn about at length).
findings.location: What section ('Restrict refs/tags/v* creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity'), AC4 (infra/terraform-github/main.tf ruleset)

findings.finding: The plan requires adding a `refs/tags/v*` Terraform ruleset 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but never defines what entity 'the release identity' refers to. The existing `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` uses `actor_type = "DeployKey"` (the Gerrit replication key) for the `main` branch lock, but the tag-gate needs a different actor — presumably a GitHub Actions runner identity, a machine user, or a new deploy key — and no `bypass_actors` specification is provided anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section (bullet 5) and AC4: 'Restrict `refs/tags/v*` creation/update/deletion/force-update to a controlled release identity via the GitHub ruleset in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph to the What section specifying the bypass actor. Given the project's model (GitHub Actions OIDC for publishing, Gerrit deploy key for main), a dedicated tag-write deploy key (write-only, separate from the replication key) or a repository-scoped GitHub Actions bypass (`actor_type = "Integration"` pointing at the Actions app) are the natural choices; pick one and record it in the plan.

findings.finding: The 'release identity' that will be the bypass actor for the new `refs/tags/v*` ruleset is never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan says 'restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity' but does not specify what actor_type, actor_id, or bypass_mode this entails. Without this, implementers may grant an overly broad bypass (e.g., a RepositoryRole:admin or an entire team) that violates least-privilege, or accidentally replicate the open-tags status quo.
findings.location: What section (bullet 5); AC4: 'defines a ruleset over refs/tags/v* restricting tag create/update/delete to the release identity'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section and AC4, name the exact bypass actor — e.g., a dedicated GitHub Actions deploy key registered as 'rebar-release-tag-push' (`actor_type = "DeployKey", bypass_mode = "always"`), mirror the deploy-key existence precondition from `main_lock`, and add a runbook step to register the key before applying. Document the key's title in `variables.tf` analogously to `deploy_key_title`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
